speaker: ALEX
text: Welcome to episode 47 of Git Going with GitHub: Fork and Contribute. I am Alex, and today we are teaching this topic as a conversation you can carry into the workshop, not as a page you have to memorize.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And I am Jamie. I will keep pulling the lesson back to real learners, real repositories, and the evidence that proves the work happened.

speaker: ALEX
text: The lesson focus is The complete fork-based open source contribution workflow from fork to upstream pull request. We will treat every step as a teachable decision, because that is what makes the skill portable.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So we should explain the why clearly enough that the steps make sense when the learner meets them later.

speaker: ALEX
text: That is it. If a listener only has audio right now, they should still get the complete concept and know what evidence would prove success.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Okay, set the room for us. What are we walking into?

speaker: ALEX
text: 1. What Is a Fork?: See also: Chapter 08: Open Source Culture for the cultural context of forking and contributing. The next useful detail is concrete: A fork is your personal copy of someone else's repository on GitHub.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How would you walk the room through that step by step?

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the learner-facing version. Most open source projects do not give every contributor write access to the main repository. Put another way, this way anyone can contribute without the maintainers giving out write access. A good GitHub workflow is like a well-run meeting: everyone knows the topic, the next action, and who has the floor.

speaker: ALEX
text: Start here: You fork the project (creates your copy). Then: You make changes on your copy. Next: You open a pull request asking the maintainers to merge your changes. The point is not speed; the point is never losing your place.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What is the one idea that makes the next few steps less mysterious?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside 2. Fork vs Clone vs Branch: these three concepts are related but different. That matters in practice: Understanding the distinctions prevents confusion.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That feels much more doable when you say it as one move.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. The magic is not speed. The magic is knowing what changed and why it matters.

speaker: ALEX
text: A solid Git habit is to know which branch you are on, what changed, and what confirmation you expect before you run the next command.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Turn that into a path someone can follow.

speaker: ALEX
text: Anchor this part on How they work together. In the fork workflow, you use all three. This is the part to say slowly: The text diagram above shows three boxes arranged left to right.

speaker: ALEX
text: Think of this as 4 checks: Fork the upstream repository to create your copy on GitHub; Clone your fork to your computer; Create a branch on your local clone for your specific change; and Push the branch to your fork and open a PR to the upstream. If one step does not match what you hear, stop there and re-orient.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What would you say to someone who is already bracing for this to be too much?

speaker: ALEX
text: Learning Cards: Fork vs Clone vs Branch has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: The parts worth keeping in working memory are these. Run git remote -v in the terminal to hear your configured remotes -- origin is your fork, upstream is the original repository. The Status Bar in VS Code shows the current branch name -- press F6 to navigate there and confirm you are on a feature branch, not main. Use Ctrl+Shift+P then "Git: Checkout to" to switch between branches; your screen reader announces each branch name in the picker. Your fork URL includes your username (e.g., github.com/your-name/repo) making it visually distinct from the upstream URL. In VS Code, the branch name in the bottom-left Status Bar confirms which branch you are working on -- zoom with Ctrl+= if it is too small. The Source Control panel heading shows the repository name to help you verify you are working in the correct clone.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Give me the sequence, because order matters here.

speaker: ALEX
text: Do not treat Tool Cards: Fork and Clone a Repository as decoration. For this workshop, you will fork the Community-Access/accessibility-agents repository. The workshop is closer to rehearsal than lecture. You hear the move, try the move, and then check what changed.

speaker: ALEX
text: First, click Fork on the repository page Create fork. Then, clone: click the green Code button copy URL paste into your local tool. After that, fork on github.com first (browser required for forking). Finally, ctrl+Shift+P Git: Clone paste your fork's URL. That small check between steps is what makes the workflow reliable.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What does the learner do first, second, and then after that?

speaker: ALEX
text: Start here: Fork on github.com first. Then: File Clone Repository select your fork from the GitHub.com tab. The sequence works because every action has a confirmation.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like gh repo fork Community-Access/accessibility-agents --clone; cd accessibility-agents. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: ALEX
text: The useful habit is simple: orient, act, verify, then continue. That pause between action and trust is part of the work.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What is the ordered workflow?

speaker: ALEX
text: If the interface shifts, On GitHub.com is still useful because the original repository at Community-Access/accessibility-agents is untouched.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is what that changes in practice. Screen reader users (NVDA/JAWS): Press B to cycle through buttons. The Fork button is near the top of the page, after the Watch and Star buttons. VoiceOver users: Use VO+U to open the Buttons rotor and navigate to "Fork.". Owner: your username. Repository name: accessibility-agents. Copy the main branch only: checked (leave this checked).

speaker: ALEX
text: Start here: Go to github.com/Community-Access/accessibility-agents. Then: Find the Fork button in the upper-right area of the page. Next: GitHub shows a "Create a new fork" page. The defaults are correct. Last: Activate Create fork. The sequence works because every action has a confirmation.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on On GitHub.com. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Walk it in order: GitHub redirects you to your fork: github.com/your-username/accessibility-agents. Keep it that plain: know where you are, make the move, check the result.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What should happen before anyone copies and runs it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Put Using GitHub CLI into plain language. This creates the fork on GitHub without cloning it locally.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like gh repo fork Community-Access/accessibility-agents --clone=false. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now it sounds like a workflow instead of a wall of instructions.

speaker: ALEX
text: That is where confidence comes from: not from never getting lost, but from knowing how to recover.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Where do you want a learner to place their attention here?

speaker: ALEX
text: The teaching point here is not the label; it is the move. Now download your fork to your computer so you can work on it.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Using VS Code. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Using VS Code has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: The path is straightforward once it is named. Step one is open VS Code. Step two is open the command palette: Ctrl+Shift+P (or Cmd+Shift+P). Step three is type "Git: Clone" and press Enter. Step four is paste your fork URL: https://github.com/your-username/accessibility-agents.git (replace your-username with your GitHub username). The point is not speed; the point is never losing your place.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we leave Using VS Code, what is the practical point?

speaker: ALEX
text: First, choose a folder (for example, Documents) and confirm. Then, when the clone finishes, VS Code offers to open the repository. Accept. Each step should leave a trace you can name.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What is the safe way to learn from that example?

speaker: ALEX
text: Using the terminal has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like git clone https://github.com/your-username/accessibility-agents.git; cd accessibility-agents. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Using GitHub Desktop. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Using GitHub Desktop has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Start here: Open GitHub Desktop. Then: Go to File, then Clone repository. Next: Select the GitHub.com tab and find your-username/accessibility-agents. Last: Choose a local path and click Clone. If one step does not match what you hear, stop there and re-orient.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What should they understand before typing anything?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside Using GitHub CLI: after cloning, your local repository has one remote called origin that points to your fork.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like gh repo clone your-username/accessibility-agents; cd accessibility-agents. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is the kind of detail that keeps a screen reader user oriented.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. The named thing - the heading, tab, field, branch, or button - is the handhold.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the moment where the page starts to make sense. Learning Cards: Cloning Your Fork has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Listen for the small confirmations in this list. Use Ctrl+Shift+P then "Git: Clone" and paste your fork URL -- VS Code announces progress and opens the repository when done. After cloning, press Ctrl+Shift+E to open the Explorer and verify the file tree loaded correctly. Run git remote -v in the terminal (Ctrl+\) to confirm origin` points to your fork URL. After cloning, the Explorer sidebar populates with the repository files -- increase sidebar width by dragging its edge for better readability. The VS Code title bar shows the repository folder name, confirming which project is open. Use Ctrl+P to quick-open any file by name if the file tree is hard to navigate at high zoom.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Give me the version that sounds like an instructor, not a manual.

speaker: ALEX
text: The reason this matters is simple: see also: Appendix E: Advanced Git for advanced remote and upstream management. The listener should be able to check this: Your clone knows about your fork (origin).

speaker: ALEX
text: The next point gives the learner a handle. Do not treat Why this matters as decoration. Without the upstream remote, you cannot.

speaker: ALEX
text: This is where the lesson becomes something you can check. Pull new changes that other contributors make to the original repository. Keep your fork up to date. Verify your changes work with the latest code.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How do you keep commands from becoming magic words?

speaker: ALEX
text: Add the upstream remote has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like git remote add upstream https://github.com/Community-Access/accessibility-agents.git. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Verify your remotes. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Put Verify your remotes into plain language. Two remotes: origin (your fork) and upstream (the original). The useful version is: In VS Code: You can also add the remote using the command palette. The interface gets easier when it becomes a set of named places instead of a wall of controls.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like git remote -v. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the learner is not behind if they stop there and check the page.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Pausing to verify is not a detour; it is how you keep control of the workflow.

speaker: JAMIE
text: If I am listening before the workshop starts, what should settle in my mind first?

speaker: ALEX
text: The teaching point here is not the label; it is the move. Never work directly on the main branch of your fork. That is the difference between guessing and knowing: Always create a feature branch for each change.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Create and switch to a new branch. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: This part earns its place because the branch name agents/your-username-my-agent follows the workshop convention for Day 2 capstone branches. That gives the learner a foothold: replace your-username with your GitHub username and my-agent with a short name for your agent.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like git checkout -b agents/your-username-my-agent. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on In VS Code. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: In VS Code has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: First, click the branch name in the bottom-left status bar (or press Ctrl+Shift+P and type "Git: Create Branch"). Then, type the branch name: agents/your-username-my-agent. After that, press Enter. VS Code creates the branch and switches to it. That is the rhythm: orient, act, verify, continue.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on In GitHub Desktop. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: In GitHub Desktop has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Start here: Click the Current Branch dropdown. Then: Click New Branch. Next: Type the branch name and click Create Branch. That small check between steps is what makes the workflow reliable.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Verify you are on the new branch. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside Verify you are on the new branch: the current branch has an asterisk ( ) next to it.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like git branch. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is a useful checkpoint before anyone starts pressing keys.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Checkpoints turn uncertainty into information.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the thread going. Anchor this part on 7. Step 5: Make Your Changes. Now you are on your feature branch and ready to work. This is the part to say slowly: For the capstone, you will create an agent file.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on General principles for contributing. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: General principles for contributing has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: These are the details that keep the idea from floating away. Keep changes focused. One branch, one purpose. If you want to make two unrelated changes, use two branches and two pull requests. Follow the project's conventions. Look at existing files for patterns in naming, formatting, and structure. Write meaningful commit messages. Describe what you changed and why. "Fix typo in README" is clear. "Update files" is not.

speaker: ALEX
text: A solid pull request habit is to separate three questions: what changed, why it changed, and what still needs review. That keeps the conversation useful instead of noisy.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Stage and commit your changes. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Stage and commit your changes has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like git add your-file.md; git commit -m "Add my-agent accessibility agent". git add.; git commit -m "Add my-agent accessibility agent". Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we leave In VS Code, what is the practical point?

speaker: ALEX
text: If someone is taking notes, this is the short list. Screen reader users: Navigate to the Source Control view. Each changed file is listed. Press Enter on a file to see the diff. Use the inline actions to stage.

speaker: ALEX
text: Start here: Open the Source Control panel: Ctrl+Shift+G (or Cmd+Shift+G). Then: Changed files appear under "Changes." Click the + icon next to each file to stage it, or click + on the "Changes" header to stage all. Next: Type your commit message in the text field at the top. Last: Press Ctrl+Enter (or Cmd+Enter) to commit. Each step should leave a trace you can name.

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the part worth saying out loud. Put Multiple commits are fine into plain language. You do not need to make all your changes in a single commit. The useful version is: In fact, smaller commits are better because they are easier to review and easier to revert if something goes wrong.

speaker: JAMIE
text: I like that because it gives people permission to slow down.

speaker: ALEX
text: That is the goal. We want the page to feel explorable, not fragile.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on 8. Step 6: Push to Your Fork. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The teaching point here is not the label; it is the move. Now push them to your fork on GitHub.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on First push (new branch). What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: This part earns its place because the -u flag sets up tracking so future pushes from this branch go to the right place automatically. That is the difference between following directions and owning the workflow.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like git push -u origin agents/your-username-my-agent. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Subsequent pushes. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Subsequent pushes has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like git push. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now shift from knowing the term to using it. Here is the learner-facing version. After committing, the Source Control panel shows a Sync Changes button (or a cloud icon with an up arrow). Put another way, alternatively, use the command palette: Ctrl+Shift+P, type "Git: Push.".

speaker: JAMIE
text: What stays the same when the tool changes?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside In GitHub Desktop: after committing, click the Push origin button in the top bar.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is the part I would want someone to say out loud while they work.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. A learner should always know what they are trying to prove before they take the next action.

speaker: ALEX
text: This is where confidence starts to build. Anchor this part on Verify on GitHub. After pushing, visit your fork on GitHub: github.com/your-username/accessibility-agents. This is the part to say slowly: You should see a banner saying "your-username-my-agent had recent pushes" with a Compare and pull request button. The durable skill is not memorizing one screen. It is knowing how to find your footing when the screen changes.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on 9. Step 7: Open a Pull Request. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The reason this matters is simple: a pull request asks the upstream maintainers to merge your branch into their repository.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on From the GitHub.com banner. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: From the GitHub.com banner has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: For a learner, the useful signals are concrete. Base repository: Community-Access/accessibility-agents. Base branch: main. Head repository: your-username/accessibility-agents. Compare branch: agents/your-username-my-agent. What your change does. Why it is useful.

speaker: ALEX
text: First, after pushing, GitHub shows a yellow banner on your fork with a Compare and pull request button. Click it. Then, GitHub opens the "Open a pull request" page. Verify. After that, write a descriptive title. Example: "Add document-contrast-checker agent.". Finally, in the body, explain. If one step does not match what you hear, stop there and re-orient.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we leave From the GitHub.com banner, what is the practical point?

speaker: ALEX
text: Start here: If the project has a PR template, fill in all the required sections. Then: Activate Create pull request. That is the rhythm: orient, act, verify, continue.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on From the Pull Requests tab. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: From the Pull Requests tab has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Start here: Go to the upstream repository: github.com/Community-Access/accessibility-agents. Then: Click the Pull requests tab. Next: Click New pull request. Last: Click compare across forks. That is the rhythm: orient, act, verify, continue.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we leave From the Pull Requests tab, what is the practical point?

speaker: ALEX
text: Walk it in order: Set the head repository to your fork and the compare branch to your feature branch; and Click Create pull request and fill in the details. That small check between steps is what makes the workflow reliable.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Using GitHub CLI. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Put Using GitHub CLI into plain language. The PR creation form is a standard web form. The useful version is: Navigate with Tab to move between fields. The interface gets easier when it becomes a set of named places instead of a wall of controls.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like gh pr create --repo Community-Access/accessibility-agents --title "Add document-contrast-checker agent" --body "Description of what the agent does and why it is useful.". Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So this is less about memorizing and more about noticing.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Once the learner can name the move, the interface becomes much less intimidating.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Learning Cards: Opening a Pull Request. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Learning Cards: Opening a Pull Request has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: The room should hear these as checkpoints. The PR form has a title input and a body textarea -- Tab between them; your screen reader announces field labels. The base and compare branch dropdowns use ARIA listbox patterns -- press Down Arrow to open and select branches. After submitting, GitHub navigates to the new PR page; press h to jump by headings and find the "Files changed" section. The "Compare and pull request" yellow banner appears at the top of your fork page after pushing -- it is a large, visible button. Use browser zoom (Ctrl+=) to enlarge the PR creation form if the text inputs are too small. The base and head repository/branch selectors appear near the top of the form -- verify these before submitting.

speaker: ALEX
text: That matters because of the next idea. This part earns its place because after you open a pull request, maintainers and peers will review your changes.

speaker: ALEX
text: That becomes easier when you listen for these cues. Approve - your PR is ready to merge. Request changes - you need to update your PR. Comment - ask questions or suggest improvements without formally requesting changes.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on How to respond to requested changes. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: How to respond to requested changes has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: First, read each review comment carefully. Understand what the reviewer is asking. Then, make the requested changes in your local clone. After that, stage, commit, and push. Finally, your new commits automatically appear in the open pull request. The reviewer is notified. Pause after each step and listen for the confirmation before moving on.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we leave How to respond to requested changes, what is the practical point?

speaker: ALEX
text: Start here: Reply to each review comment explaining what you changed, or ask clarifying questions if the feedback is unclear. The point is not speed; the point is never losing your place.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like git add modified-file.md; git commit -m "Address review feedback: improve guardrails section"; git push. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: ALEX
text: This is where the talk moves from concept to action. Here is the learner-facing version. The Day 1 connection: You practiced code review in Chapter 15. Put another way, the same principles apply here, but now you are on the other side -- receiving feedback instead of giving it. A good GitHub workflow is like a well-run meeting: everyone knows the topic, the next action, and who has the floor.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part that makes the next action easier. Thank the reviewer. They spent time reading your code. Address every comment. Even if you disagree, explain your reasoning. Do not take feedback personally. Review is about the code, not about you. Ask questions. If you do not understand a comment, ask. Reviewers expect questions from new contributors.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on 11. Keeping Your Fork in Sync. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside 11. Keeping Your Fork in Sync: over time, other people's changes get merged into the upstream repository. That matters in practice: Your fork does not update automatically.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Sync from the command line. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Sync from the command line has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like Make sure you are on main; git checkout main; Download the latest changes from upstream; git fetch upstream; Merge upstream changes into your local main; git merge upstream/main; Push the updated main to your fork; git push origin main. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Sync from GitHub.com. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Sync from GitHub.com has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: The path is straightforward once it is named. Step one is go to your fork on GitHub. Step two is if your fork is behind the upstream, GitHub shows a banner: "This branch is X commits behind Community-Access:main.". Step three is click Sync fork, then Update branch. That is the rhythm: orient, act, verify, continue.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Sync from GitHub CLI. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Sync from GitHub CLI has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like gh repo sync your-username/accessibility-agents. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on When to sync. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: When to sync has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: The useful version is not abstract; it sounds like this. Before starting new work: Always sync before creating a new feature branch. This ensures your branch starts from the latest code. Before opening a PR: Sync and merge main into your feature branch to check for conflicts before asking for review. Periodically: If you are working on a long-running branch, sync weekly to avoid large conflicts.

speaker: ALEX
text: Hold that next to this. Learning Cards: Keeping Your Fork in Sync has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: A few details make that real. Run git fetch upstream then git merge upstream/main in the terminal -- listen for "Already up to date" or a merge summary announcing new commits. Use git status after syncing to confirm your local main is not behind the upstream. On GitHub.com, the "Sync fork" button is near the top of your fork page; press Tab to reach it and Enter to activate. On GitHub.com, the "Sync fork" button appears below the repository description with a dropdown showing how many commits behind you are. After syncing, the Status Bar in VS Code shows no up/down arrows next to the branch name, confirming you are in sync. If a merge conflict occurs during sync, VS Code highlights conflicts with colored backgrounds (green = yours, blue = upstream).

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on 12. The Fork Workflow Checklist. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The teaching point here is not the label; it is the move. Use this checklist every time you contribute to a repository you do not own.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here are the anchors worth keeping. [ ] Fork the repository on GitHub. [ ] Clone your fork locally. [ ] Add the upstream remote (git remote add upstream URL). [ ] Create a feature branch (git checkout -b branch-name). [ ] Make your changes, stage, and commit. [ ] Push your branch to your fork (git push -u origin branch-name).

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Cleaning up after merge. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: This part earns its place because after your PR is merged, delete the feature branch. That gives the learner a foothold: GitHub also offers a "Delete branch" button on the merged PR page. That is the difference between following directions and owning the workflow.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like Delete the local branch; git checkout main; git branch -d agents/your-username-my-agent; Delete the remote branch on your fork; git push origin --delete agents/your-username-my-agent. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How should someone ask for help in a way that gets them unstuck faster?

speaker: ALEX
text: 13. If You Get Stuck: Next: Chapter 19: Accessibility Agents Back: Chapter 17: Issue Templates Related appendices: Appendix E: Advanced Git Appendix O: Branch Protection.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What should people carry with them after this?

speaker: ALEX
text: Carry the map. Know what page or tool you are in, know which action you are taking, and know what confirmation should follow. If the confirmation is missing, pause. That pause is not wasted time; it is professional judgment.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is a better way to say it than just follow the steps.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Steps matter, but understanding wins. That is episode 47. Next in the series is episode 48, where we keep building the same contributor muscles.
